data.title: YPF Sociedad Anonima: An Asymmetric Energy Play Tied To Argentina's Normalization
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Argentina-based state-owned oil company YPF Sociedad Anónima (YPF) has as its investment thesis not a simple proxy for Brent, but rather as an asymmetric asset anchored in three key vectors: (1) Argentina's macro normalization; (2) operational execution in shale; and (3) a long-term option in LNG.

In the short term, ADR prices tend to be dominated by Argentina's country risk and implicit cost of capital.

In the medium term, value creation tends to come from capex conversion in growing volumes, structurally low costs in Vaca Muerta, and reducing dependence on the price of a barrel of oil.

In the long term, the monetization of gas via exports and LNG adds a relevant optionality to the thesis, which is not yet reflected in the valuation. Oil, in this context, ends up acting only as a residual beta and not as the central driver of the thesis.

YPF’s Business Model and the Core Drivers of Value Creation

For those unfamiliar with YPF's thesis, it's Argentina's largest energy company, government-controlled (owning 51% of the company), vertically integrated (E&P, refining, and fuel distribution), and deeply linked to the country's energy security.

These factors give YPF a dominant position in the domestic fuel and gas market, which helps it to typically sustain operating margins in normal cycles.

As the backbone of the thesis, the main return vector is the expansion of unconventional hydrocarbons in Vaca Muerta, which is one of the largest shale gas/shale oil reserves in the world, with very significant export potential. As a result, growing productive investments in E&P are likely to increase extracted volumes and revenues over the coming years.

YPF has therefore made some recent strategic moves. Over the last couple of years, with Argentina still in a severe economic crisis, global majors and independents have reduced exposure to the country and prioritized exposure to portfolios with lower sovereign risk. As a result, YPF increased its participation in core blocks and concentrated its capital where breakeven was lowest and well productivity was highest. Thus, Vaca Muerta went from being a "shared" portfolio to an asset more anchored in YPF.

In addition to growth via Vaca Muerta, it's also worth noting that YPF has been divesting non-core assets—for example, the sale of Profertil (fertilizers)—in order to concentrate capital on oil and gas.

When we look at YPF's P&L today, it's mostly explained by shale oil from Vaca Muerta and not necessarily by “conventional” oil or gas.

Although oil still represents the bulk of gross revenue, marginal EBITDA comes from shale, where production grew in the most recent quarter by 35% vs. the same period last year (170 kbbl/d), and costs fell structurally (core-hub lifting cost fell to US$8.8/boe, signaling a 45% YoY drop).

This combination of volume and cost explains the resilience of YPF's EBITDA reported in 3Q25, which came in at US$1.36 billion, up 21% YoY, and highlights how upstream shale is the company's current main engine.

In second place comes downstream and gas. Refining, for YPF, comes in as a cash stabilizer for the company, with 97% utilization, but with compressed margins (R&M adj. EBITDA of US$5.9/bbl, down 50% YoY), which ends up limiting the influence on incremental profit.

On the gas side, despite recent stable volumes (~38 Mm³/d) and a lower immediate contribution to EBITDA due to low domestic prices, this is the most relevant asset for the future value of YPF's equity, mainly because it supports the LNG export thesis and hard currency revenues.

Thus, at present, it can be said that the order of influence in the P&L ends up with (1) shale oil; (2) downstream; and (3) gas.

YPF’s Equity Through a Multi-Vector Lens

As the order of relevance in YPF's P&L becomes clear, I believe that there's still one price factor that today should be correlated first with the company's ADR: Argentine macro risk.

Only after that do I believe that shale execution (volume and costs), LNG optionality, and marginally Brent spot tend to be considerable drivers for the thesis, in that respective order.

That said, YPF’s share price can be conceptually decomposed as follows: (1) Argentina Regime; (2) shale execution; (3) LNG option; and (4) beta oil residual.

And each term tends to have a dominant horizon. For example, in the short term (six to 12 months), the haircut dominates. In the medium term (12 to 18 months), execution (volume and costs) begins to dominate. In the long term (two to five years), the LNG option becomes the main "call."

Argentine Macro Regime

The price of oil is no longer the main marginal driver of YPF equity, but rather Argentine macro risk.

As the following graph shows, while Brent spot prices have fallen by around 19% in the last 12 months, YPF has fallen by more than 20% in the period, even though there has been a clear operational improvement. More importantly, especially from 2H25 onwards, there are windows pointing to the fact that YPF has shown significant inflections, showing that oil has indeed acted as a kind of secondary floor.

These inflections, however, coincide much more with a movement of compression and repricing of country risk, captured in this case by the sovereign bond in USD, which grew 9% in the same period.

That said, my reading is that in the current government regime, marginal variations in Brent spot prices tend to be less relevant to YPF's equity than necessarily a change in the company's implicit cost of capital. As the thesis is based on future NAV (shale and gas) and not necessarily on immediate cash flows and dividends, I see the market more inclined to price the denominator first (market risk) and relegate Brent to a secondary position of influence on YPF's ADR price.

For more specific details on Argentina's macroeconomic direction, I recently wrote an article on Banco BBVA Argentina (BBAR) in which I went into more detail on the country's economic developments under the presidency of Javier Milei.

Shale Execution

YPF's operational evolution throughout 2025 makes it quite clear that the sensitivity of shale results is much more linked to volumes and costs than to spot Brent.

If we look at the results from 1Q25 to 3Q25, shale oil production rose from 147 kbbl/d to 170 kbbl/d (about 16% over the last nine months and more than 35% YoY). At the same time, the total lifting cost fell from US$15.3/boe to US$8.8/boe, a cumulative reduction of ~40%. Even as Brent retreated to ~US$60 in 3Q25, shale's implicit operating margin expanded significantly, reinforcing that marginal EBITDA creation has been dominated by efficiency and scale gains.

And it's precisely this dynamic that helps explain the 21% sequential rise in adjusted EBITDA in 3Q25, despite a weaker price environment, while FCF remained negative.

Throughout 2025, capex has been deliberately targeted to convert investment into volumes and reduce unit cost, which temporarily raises leverage (net debt/EBITDA from 1.8x in 1Q25 to 2.1x in 3Q25), but with clear evidence of operational return.

In other words, YPF's negative FCF reflects a choice of growth, not a deterioration in assets. As shale production accelerates and lifting costs fall quarter on quarter, YPF is beginning to be priced according to the positive sensitivity of EBITDA to its execution, and no longer as a simple beta of Brent.

This operating pattern is in direct contrast to my bearish view of Petrobras (PBR) - Brazil's state-owned oil giant - which I also invite you to read, where it also shows my view of Brent's direction for 2026.

While YPF is in an investment cycle in which growing volumes and falling costs reduce its dependence on the price of a barrel, Petrobras continues to expand capex at a time when equity sensitivity remains strongly tied to Brent, given the weight of high dividends, implicit commitments, and less operational flexibility.

In my view, YPF has used the cycle far more constructively - buying efficiency and future EBITDA - while other majors, such as Petrobras, have been increasing economic leverage precisely in a lateralized oil environment.

LNG Optionality

With regard to gas, in Argentina, domestic gas is subsidized, which is why it doesn't contribute substantially to YPF's EBITDA. Therefore, the optionality of gas and NGLs does not yet appear in the company's quarterly operating results, simply because there's no materialized export yet.

However, YPF already structures this segment as part of its strategic matrix (Gas & Power and LNG & Integrated Gas) and has projections of future net energy exports and growing production volumes at Vaca Muerta and the generation of structural surpluses in the face of domestic demand.

In YPF's long-term materials, the company and the Argentine government indicate the country's transition to a position of net energy exporter by the end of the decade. There's an LNG (liquefied natural gas) project structured in phases, with an initial capacity estimated at around ~6 MTPA and the possibility of expansion as execution milestones are reached.

This LNG export project, in this case involving international players such as Adnoc/Eni (E), has ~US$50 billion in planned investments, targeting foreign markets, which was another even more transformational point.

Argentina has had a historical problem where there has been abundant gas on the one hand, but no export infrastructure, dependence on the domestic market, and regulated prices on the other. The LNG project involves liquefaction on the coast, with exports to Europe and Asia and long-term contracts (take-or-pay). As a result, Argentine gas now has an international price and is no longer at risk of domestic control.

The price of YPF ADR in this case tends to react to milestones (approved FID, partners, dedicated pipeline). In other words, it's an event-driven proxy, where each approved phase increases the expected NAV and reduces the probability discount.

Final Remarks and Risks

By decomposing YPF's price into the vectors of (1) Argentine regime; (2) shale play; (3) LNG option and; (4) residual oil beta, it's clear that the market does not evaluate YPF as a simple oil proxy. In the short term, my view is that equity will be mostly responsive to Argentina's macro regime and the country's implicit cost of capital—factors that I see as structurally bullish (albeit with volatility).

In the medium term, the sustainability of any re-rating depends on the continuity of the execution in shale, with increasing volumes in decline. Recent data shows the thesis validated (volumes up, costs down, and EBITDA up, even with weaker Brent), in addition to YPF's proven capacity for capex in kbbl/d and efficiency, reducing dependence on the price of a barrel. So, here too, I see it as structurally bullish.

In the long term, the LNG optionality represents an asymmetric bullish vector of appreciation, where materialization depends on execution milestones and not on quarterly results. Brent, on the other hand, lateralized and potentially with a neutral/bearish bias in the middle of the current cycle, acts only as a residual beta within this structure.

Therefore, the main risk in the YPF case at present may be attributed not to the asset but to the regime. The upside, on the other hand, comes from execution and the asymmetry of optionality.

Finally, YPF's valuation is not stretched when read by the right multiples, but it is also no longer “deeply distressed” as it was in the recent past. Forward EV/EBITDA at 4.8x is still 27% below the sector and reflects a relevant macro haircut. The forward price-to-cash flow at 2.7x, around 55% below the sector, also shows that the market is not pricing in a full normalization.

This shows me that, in a way, the market is not paying for LNG, nor for a full normalization of Argentina, and is only partially paying for shale execution. That said, I remain constructive on the YPF thesis, both in the short, medium, and long term.

data.title: TEGNA: Attractive Amidst Regulatory Uncertainty
data.description:
While shares are up 3% from last year, it has been a bumpy ride for investors in TEGNA Inc. (TGNA). After weak performance in H1 2025 amid fears of an advertising slowdown, the stock popped when Nexstar (NXST) announced a deal to buy the company. Since then, we have seen TEGNA shares steadily drift lower as fears have grown about potential regulatory hurdles to the deal. I last covered shares in August, rating TEGNA a “Buy,” but the stock is down 5% since then. With shares at a meaningful discount to the deal price, now is a good time to revisit TEGNA to see if investors should buy into weakness or exit shares.

NXST Is Paying $22 In Cash For TGNA

First, to recap the terms of the deal, in mid-August, Nexstar agreed to acquire all of TEGNA’s outstanding shares for $22 in cash. Frankly, this was a bit less than I expected, and TGNA’s decision to focus on an all-cash offer may have limited the price somewhat. However, from this point forward, the analysis is more straightforward than if investors were to be receiving NXST stock, whose value can change daily. I would also note that TEGNA shareholders have already approved this deal, removing one potential variable to the successful close of the merger.

With shares closing on Wednesday at $18.91, investors have 16.3% upside to the deal price. Plus, TEGNA can continue to pay its regular dividend, providing a 2.6% yield. The companies are targeting a close in the second half of 2026, so if the deal goes through, this would be a compelling total return opportunity for investors over a 6-9 month window. In a deal with limited regulatory risk, I would expect shares to trade within ~5% of the deal price. Assuming the stock would trade closer to $16-$17 in the immediate aftermath of a failed deal (similar to its pre-deal level), this suggests the market is pricing in just a 50%-60% probability of the deal going through.

FCC Cap Is The Key Uncertainty

That brings us to the regulatory backdrop. In early November, the Department of Justice requested information on the proposed transaction, which is typical for a deal of this magnitude and not something I would reach much into (positively or negatively). Ultimately, the regulator that matters here the most is the FCC because current FCC rules bar a single broadcaster from owning stations that reach more than 39% of households, which the combined company would surpass. Most of the broadcast stocks, like TEGNA, rallied in the aftermath of President Trump’s victory in the 2024 election in the belief his Administration would likely roll back these rules. In fact, Nexstar management speaking at a conference actually said, “we wouldn’t be contemplating this transaction if he [Trump] weren’t in the White House.”

However, this view around deregulation was thrown into uncertainty when the President posted his opposition to lifting the cap in November on Truth Social. Now, the president is very active on social media, and not every post becomes policy. His post also focused on not expanding the broadcast networks. This deal does not change the reach of ABC or NBC—it would simply change who owns the affiliate station. Notably, on Jan. 14, there were reports that the FCC Chairman Brendan Carr was still planning to lift the cap, consistent with his long-standing position, despite the President’s prior post.

Nexstar Is Positioned To Close The Deal, In My View

Now, to be clear, in this article, I am making no political statements or saying what policy is “better” than another; however, in a case like this when a regulatory decision will determine how a stock performs, one must consider the political backdrop. To that end, it is notable that Nexstar was one of the broadcasters that chose to preempt Jimmy Kimmel’s show during the controversy in September, which aligned the network with the Administration. Speaking at the December conference, Nexstar reiterated its belief the deal would be completed. I would also note that the FCC could choose to grant Nexstar a waiver to complete the deal without eliminating the cap entirely.

Fundamentally, there are also clear competitive reasons to remove this cap. This cap was intended to limit concentration of power in media, but the media backdrop is very different today than decades ago. Broadcast networks face competition from cable news, digital outlets, social media, and other platforms. Alphabet’s YouTube can essentially reach every American household, as can Facebook. The risk of a broadcaster having a true information monopoly is very remote in my view, with or without this cap, which is why I believe it will be eventually repealed or diluted.

Termination Fee Provides Some Support

Now, some of these regulatory hurdles are likely to bring back bad memories of long-time TEGNA shareholders because its attempt to sell itself to an arm of Apollo (APO) for $24 failed due to regulatory review deals by the FCC during the Biden Administration. That deal was ultimately terminated, though one offset was that TEGNA received a $136 million breakup fee, which enabled a large share repurchase program. Still, the stock spent much of the time since then stuck in the mid-teens before reaching this deal with Nexstar.

Having gone through this once before, TEGNA insisted upon a termination fee if the deal cannot be completed. Under the merger agreement, Nexstar has to pay TEGNA $125 million if it cannot get regulatory approval by Aug. 18, 2026. Either company has the right to extend this deadline one time for three months, so the true deadline here is Nov. 18, 2026. This payment does help to cushion the potential downside if a deal fails and is ~$0.75/share based on its shares outstanding as of the third quarter.

TGNA Is Cheap Even If The Deal Is Blocked

Now, if the deal gets approved, the outcome for TGNA is clear—shares will be sold to NXST for $22. However, investors must also consider the downside if a deal fails. In Q3, TEGNA earned $0.33, and revenue fell by 19% to $651 million. This large revenue decline is because in 2024 there were the Olympics and a presidential election, which each drive substantial ad revenue. Local networks generate much more revenue in election years, which is why I generally view their financials on a four-year basis, given the calendar year seasonality.

This revenue decline was consistent with my expectations. Distribution revenue fell by 1% to $358 million as consumers continue to cut the cord, partially offset by price increases. Non-political ad revenue fell 12%, primarily due to the Olympics; still, the macro environment has been challenging for the ad market, with companies pulling back on their marketing budgets. Political revenue fell by 92% to $10 million. As a result, EBITDA fell by 52% to $141 million. TEGNA has generated $222 million of free cash flow so far this year.

I was targeting $300 million of free cash flow this year, and I expect TEGNA to come within $15 million of that level, depending on working capital. Essentially, while facing ongoing declines, TEGNA is performing as I expected. TEGNA has suspended issuing guidance, which is typical when a company is being acquired. As a reminder, 2025 is seasonally its worst year in a four-year cycle. 2026 will improve with the midterms; then 2027 will soften, and 2028 will be the strongest (all else equal) due to the presidential election. As such, run-rate free cash is higher than this $300 million, and I see ~$450 million as its average level.

TEGNA ended Q3 with $233 million of cash and 2.9x net leverage. We are likely to see its cash position build over 2026 as it will not be repurchasing stock while waiting for the Nexstar deal to close. If the deal fell through, it would have to added $125 million of cash as well from Nexstar. TEGNA could easily do a ~$300 million buyback immediately, which would reduce the share count by ~10%.

The run-rate business would have ~$2.75-$3.00 of free cash flow, so even with shares at ~$19 today, TEGNA has a 15+% free cash flow yield. While the business is declining, this is very attractive, especially as I see the decline being closer to 2%-3% than 5%, as evidenced by just a 1% drop in distribution revenue. If the deal is blocked, shares would almost certainly fall in a knee-jerk fashion, but even at $19, I actually think TEGNA is positioned to deliver a long-term double-digit return to shareholders.

Shares Are A "Buy"

As such, I think this decline in TEGNA stock has created a compelling opportunity. With FCC Chairman Carr's preference for removing the cap and Nexstar’s political maneuvering, I expect the deal to close and assign a 75% probability. This will give investors an 18% return, including the dividend, over just six to nine months. Even if the deal were to get blocked, TEGNA’s building cash position and termination fee would support a large buyback, and shares are cheap enough, that there is limited long-term downside even if they could briefly dip to $17. I recommend buying TEGNA here and rate shares a “Buy.”

data.title: SiTime: Valuation Is Holding Me Back From Turning Bullish
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for SiTime Corp (SITM) is a hold rating. SITM is a high-quality business in the semiconductor industry, with clear structural tailwinds from AI infrastructure and data centers. However, this strength is already priced in by the market, I believe. While the fundamentals remain excellent, the valuation leaves little room for error. At current levels, the risk-reward is not attractive.

Company Description

SITM offers a strong solution to precision timing. Unlike the legacy method, which was quartz-based, the SITM solution is silicon-native and structurally superior. One way to visualize this is to think of the processor as the brain of an electronic system, and the timing device is the heartbeat. The system will not work without the heartbeat (timing). The constraints with the quartz-based method are that it is fragile, bulky, and limited in flexibility in modern designs.

SITM MEMS-based silicon timing architecture solves all these. By using standard semiconductor manufacturing, SITM delivers programmable timing devices that are smaller and more durable (to heat, vibration, and electromagnetic interference). Operation-wise, SITM is a fabless designer. Its sole focus is purely on performance, customization, and iteration, and not production. With ~90% market share in MEMS timing, SITM is an effective monopoly in this niche segment.

AI & Data Center

SITM should continue to print rapid growth because of its exposure to the AI and data center industry, specifically through its Communications, Enterprise & Data Center segment, which saw revenue grow 115% y/y in Q3 2025 (6th straight quarter of triple-digit growth). The crux here is the AI infrastructure arms race. As data centers scale into massive AI clusters, especially those built around platforms like NVIDIA’s Blackwell (which is extremely expensive), there is no tolerance for timing error. Take a second to think of how complex the entire architecture is. You have thousands of GPUs, switches, and optical interconnects stitched together, and all of them must tick in the same timing. As I said, timing is mission-critical. Even a 1/1000 shift in timing can cascade into huge problems like latency, data corruption, or underutilized compute.

This technical complexity, then, creates a massive opportunity for SITM. In the past, timing in a standard server was cheap and simple. But today, in a fully configured AI rack, the demand for extreme precision is so high that SITM content per rack has jumped to the hundreds of dollars (source: 28th Annual Needham Conference). And I expect this dollar amount to go even higher with the upcoming shift to 1.6T optical modules. Put simply, to move data at that speed, the time window to read each bit of information becomes incredibly tiny. You would need an ultra-precise and steady signal to capture the data. Because these are way more complex and harder to make, I believe it would justify SITM to sell them at much higher prices.

Apple as a Strategic Catalyst

The next growth catalyst is SITM’s relationship with Apple (AAPL). SITM has secured critical design wins for Apple’s internal modem program, which I believe carries significant value for SITM as a business. Most readers are aware of how obsessed AAPL is with saving space inside the iPhone (started by Steve Jobs), and when they decided to build their own internal modem, they hit a physical limit. The traditional quartz timing components were simply too big and fragile to fit into the iPhone (literally cannot fit). Here comes SITM with its MEMS chips that are up to 1,000 times smaller than quartz, which effectively solved this problem. Beyond the fact that this shows MEMS chips can actually replace quartz-based methods, the other notable aspect is that AAPL didn't just put SITM in the iPhone for a minor purpose; they put it in the radio (which is the most sensitive and critical part of the phone). The way I see it, if SITM is good enough for AAPL (one of the largest tech players in the world), it should be able to meet the demand for most other customers.

The financial impact is massive, and the number should become more apparent in SITM’s P&L as this new internal modem rolls across the other iPhone lineups. Therefore, I would say this deal is not really a one-off content win, but one that allows SITM to ride on AAPL's long-term product roadmap. Some might argue that AAPL could easily switch, but I don’t think so. Once a custom timing solution is designed to fit AAPL’s complex architecture, it becomes very sticky. Remember, this isn't a commodity. SITM’s MEMS chips are up to 1,000x smaller than quartz. AAPL has likely designed its entire internal modem board around this; hence, to switch back to a competitor (who would likely use bulkier quartz), AAPL would physically have to redesign the motherboard to find space (that doesn't exist). Furthermore, validating a new timing source would mean a delay for its upcoming products.

The Titan & Aura Expansion

SITM is also expanding its total addressable market, which enhances the long-term growth runway. The launch of the Titan resonator platform is a prime example of how it is doing so. Historically, SITM sold oscillators (this is basically bundled solutions that include both a resonator and a chip), and that left a gap in the whole product (the resonator). With Titan, SITM can now close this gap and address this ~$4 billion market (source: Q3 2025 earnings call). My belief is SITM will win a good chunk of share here since it is already selling the oscillators, and the trust has already been established between it and the customers. But more importantly, the MEMS resonator can be embedded directly inside a customer’s chip package. This is a huge value proposition for semiconductor designers because it removes the need for an external quartz crystal on the circuit board. In turn, this simplifies design layouts and saves space, which is valuable for IoT products (like smartwatches that have strict physical limitations).

This is also margin accretive, as management has indicated Titan products should carry gross margins of >60% (recent gross margin is ~59%). As such, when these products scale, they should lift the overall business margin profile.

The other way SITM is expanding its TAM is by moving up the value chain. Through the Aura Semiconductor acquisition, SITM obtained the necessary IP to offer complete clock systems and not just sell the individual timing components. This improves the relationship with customers, I believe, because SITM effectively becomes a turnkey solution partner that can address a complete solution. There is no need for customers to check out other solution providers (saving time and easy to manage).

Valuation

Overall, I believe there is good visibility that SITM can continue to grow, backed by structural AI and data center spending tailwinds, the APPL catalyst, and TAM expansion. Because of the small earnings base today, I used revenue growth to better value the business. Relative to peers, SITM has the best growth profile, and it makes sense that it trades at a premium. Peers trade on average ~5x forward revenue with low-to-mid-teens revenue growth, while SITM trades close to 19x with >30% revenue CAGR over the next two years.

Using consensus estimates, which have been historically accurate (in fact SITM always beats), SITM should do $700 million in revenue in FY28. The question is what multiple it deserves at that point when the percentage growth has lowered due to the law of large numbers. In FY28, the expected revenue growth rate is in the mid-20%, which is somewhere between Rambus and Semtech. Take the average multiple of the two; at ~9x forward revenue multiple, this implies SITM is worth an enterprise value of $6.3 billion. Add all the cash that SITM is expected to generate (about $230 million or so between Y26 and FY27) and the current cash it has ($809 million), and the market cap sums up to ~$7.4 billion, or a share price of $281. You could argue that SITM deserves to trade at a higher multiple because of what’s going on, but I think this shows that valuation is not cheap, and investors cannot get attractive returns without making aggressive assumptions.

Conclusion

SIM is doing almost everything right operationally. It benefits from secular AI-driven tailwinds, is deeply embedded in Apple’s roadmap, and continues to expand its TAM. What holds me back is not the fundamentals, but the valuation. At ~19x forward revenue, much of this upside seems to be already priced in. I do think SITM is a high-quality compounder, but I recommend a hold rating, waiting for a more attractive entry point.

data.title: Old Republic: Decent Overall Performance, But Still Overvalued Into 2026
data.description:
In this article, I'll be providing an update on my thesis for Old Republic International (ORI), an insurance company, which I last updated on in January of 2025, in an article you can find here. The performance since then for the company has been solid - above the SPY - but recent volatility in the share price in my view confirms my view at the time - that the valuation might have been overvalued.

The past few years have seen a peaking of many insurance companies' valuations, as well as reinsurance company valuations. In general, it's fair to say that I exited many of these investments a bit early, as I seem to want to do in general as well. It's one of my investment shortcomings as an analyst and investor that I try to work on - but it's tricky - because about 30-40% of the time, if I look at my investment volume over the past 5 years, I in fact do not exit too early, but just in time.

Therefore, balancing my tendency to get out too early is hard. With Old Republic, it was, in my view, not as tricky. The reason is that for over 15 years, ORI has been a company that has traded at a relatively discounted valuation, and for a good reason. The reason is that during the GFC and for many years after, the company saw negative AEPS/Earnings. It wasn't actually until 2013 that ORI once again saw positive earnings and "climbed back up". Look below to see an example of how "bad" things were at the time.

This is the problem with some insurance investors/investments on the market today, in my view. They seem to have forgotten the potential volatility of these companies and the underlying markets, despite numerous reminders over the past 10-20 years.

Now, ORI is part of the US P&C market. Because of that, it's fair to say that the company is part of the ongoing bifurcation of the sector.

What does this mean?

That's what this article and update are going to be about - and to show you what this means, you should be careful about the valuation.

Old Republic International - Upside and fundamentals

So, the two main markets in standard insurance are Property & Casualty, P&C, and Life/Health, L&H. Some companies do both, most do only one of the things, or at least focus on the things. The reason for this division is simple - they are different segments with different business models and risk assessment models. P&C is short-term, L&H is a long-term oriented policy, and risk assessments are predictable in L&H, because they use statistics, comorbidity rates with actuarial tables. In P&C, you have to assess based on frequency and severity specific to geographies, areas, and other factors, while also recently taking into consideration things like climate changes (in the industry known as 'catastrophic risk').

For our purpose as investors, P&C is also considered a transactional business. The agents for companies in the sector do get a commission, but also earn based on renewals every time clients pay their premiums. In the L&H sector, it's entirely different - renewals are usually nonexistent, and upfront commissions are comparatively large.

Keep this in mind next time you're negotiating your insurance, and keep the agent's (if there is one) motivations in mind.

Now that we know the difference, what's this bifurcation in P&C?

This means that there's one area which is doing well (depending on your perspective), while another that's doing - to not use an extreme descriptive term - less well.

Property insurance is doing well in the US, where ORI operates. The reasons for this are several. There's significant capital available in the form of both Cat bonds as well as traditional insurance, meaning that there's a battle for "good" risk coverages in many areas. There's an exception to this - natCat areas like the Midwest and the West Coast are seeing persistent high rates for Property insurance, but outside of these, there are significant rate decreases at the point of renewal. This is not necessarily all positive for Old Republic, but it's better than the alternative, below.

Casualty market insurance is not all that well. The US is a very litigious market, and recent large cases that have seen significant jury awards as well as high legal costs are driving up claims and policy costs.

This may be good for some policyowners, but the aggregate effect is a significant rise in costs. For commercial/industrial players, the mere act of getting good coverage has become more complicated due to the need for more "layering" of insurance, as insurance players are moving away from "umbrella" policies. Then there's automotive.

Automotive is, again, especially in the US, going up because of significant medical cost inflation as well as legal cost inflation (as well as repair cost inflation due to complexity). This is despite better safety tech.

So if you want to characterize this market as "not that bad", that's anyone's prerogative - I would consider it to be quite complex, and the thesis for 2026E for any insurance company, but especially a US-based one, to be incredibly complex.

3Q25 was basically a continuation of the company's ongoing income and cost trends - solid income growth, good RoE, and a so-so dividend of 2.7% if you buy at this particular time.

ORI is performing according to its solid business model, but I do not believe it's able to rise above industry trends. Its high valuation is also continually driven by ongoing share buybacks - $44M as of late, with over $900M+ remaining - so expect the inflation in price to remain in part because of this trend. The company's recent results confirm the above trend in premium growth - what between 2016-2021 was relative stable, has risen significantly since 2022. Also, compared to the risks mentioned above, the company is especially exposed to commercial auto, at over 40% of its NPE.

So, overall, I don't view ORI as a "Low risk" investment any longer. This is because the combination of exposure, the market trends and dynamics we're seeing for the 2026-2028E period, as well as this company's significant valuation inflation, driven in part by company actions (such as buybacks), and in part from general valuation trends (the market being priced highly).

Because the full-year results are close (in about a week), I'll also be providing an earnings preview for the company, based on my assumptions for the sector and for the company. Despite the risks and headwinds that I note in both the above and my valuation segment (as well as the separate risks & Considerations), I don't believe that 4Q25 or FY results will bring about anything major. An "as-expected" performance or trend is my current forecast for the company, with a total EPS forecast of $3.3/share for the full year, based on a revenue of about $8.9B, which would come to about ~8% YoY - about $2.3B specifically for 4Q. Main drivers are continued growth in speciality and workers comp, but I expect the title insurance segment to face continued challenging trends related to the above-mentioned challenges in real estate, not only affordability, but also Natcat. I expect this to impact the combined ratio and expect a 20-40 bps decrease (a decrease in the context of combined ratio means an increase to about 95.5% - in combined ratio terms, lower is better). This strengthens my stance that ORI is not a "safer" investment, despite good results.

Valuation trends for ORI, despite a good 3Q25, look as follows:

Valuation for Old Republic International - upside possible

Fundamentals for this company have no similarities to what they once were during their "bad" years. It has a strong bond/stock portfolio, strong reserves, low debt, good capital, and strong management. It will take very strong negative macro trends for the company to "go down" from here. The thing is, the market is setting up for that to be the case (at least potentially).

If we assume that the forecasts are accurate or valid, then we can expect no more than 2-4% growth on average between 2026-2027E. The reason why things might be better than that is that over the last 10 years, the company has been able to beat its forecast estimates most of the time. However, due to a period that's been favorable to most large P&C insurers, this is not exactly a hard feat during those past years. This is one of those scenarios where I'm uncertain about the value of backwards-looking statistics and trends.

The fact is that the company's current valuation is about 12-13x P/E on average. This is next to a historical 5-year average of 10x, about a 20-30% premium, or a 20-year average of 11-12x, about a 5-10% premium. And this is even after the company recently dipped from $46 down to $41/share again, an even clearer overvaluation (in my estimate).

My PT in my last article for ORI came to $27/share, with a fair value of about $37/share. That means that ORI is below my target and FV here. To those that argue that my PT/FV is too low, the fair value represented at the time, and still does, a 2027E 10x P/E, coming to about $36-$37/share. Not only is this, in my estimate, fair given the company's historical risk and automotive exposure, it's also fair given the fact that there are insurance companies on the market that you could buy at significantly lower multiples. Neither the company BV (below $30/share) nor other factors justify a higher valuation to me here.

As of this article, I make an allowance for ORI to outperform further - I raise my FV to an 11x forward P/E, to about $39/share, with a PT of $29/share, but that's about it. If you were to invest here, based on a 5-year average, your potential rate of return would be negative.

Even if you were to forecast at 12-13x P/E, the current valuation, it wouldn't be any higher than single digits. For that reason, I am still at a "HOLD" in my rating here, despite a relatively significant upgrade in my targets and assumptions.

There are risks to investing in Old Republic International - they are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The US insurance market may be more profitable in some ways, but it also comes with more risks and challenges than the EU market. The accounting for the litigious US legal system, which is driving policy inflation and complexity, is not for the faint-hearted. It doesn't mean that I am no longer investing in US insurance (i have several investments), but it does mean I'm doing so much more conservatively, and only at low multiples.

Risks include, aside from things mentioned above, also things like "The AI of insurance". Many of the bigger players are moving towards operational AI instead of small-scale use of AI for insurance, which means integrating this into the process flow for underwriting and claims adjusting - but this is untested ground. It may fail, or it may rise.

My overall view for the insurance market going into 2026E is not a positive one, especially in the US. Due to the trends mentioned above, I forecast a 5-15% decrease in pricing not only in commercial property, but in other sub-sectors as well. One of the few sectors that's likely to rise, in my view, is workers comp - and because ORI is strong in this sector, this could be an upside to consider (again, at the right price).

Thesis

Old Republic International is an overvalued insurer, albeit one with excellent fundamentals. It has a well-covered yield and low debt, and the results are promising, despite my "HOLD" rating. The company's 20-year history shows a bit of volatility for extended periods of time (especially during the negative period following GFC, several years of underperformance) - but the company has righted and adjusted since, and overall, I view the company as "safe" - at least from a fundamental point of view.

In my previous articles (2024 and 2025), I made it clear that I rotated my position in the Old Republic into what I viewed as better investments. I am still not invested in ORI here. My alternative investments to ORI have performed better than ORI even now, as I've detailed in previous articles, in things like chocolate (Barry Callebaut), and others.

The company is, thanks to recent trends in trading, still overvalued. I increase my PT to $29/share, which still makes it a "HOLD" here as of January 2026. My fair value for the company is at about $39/share, which also makes this company overvalued. I view it as a rotation target.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized):

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic conservative upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of $29/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: Match Group: Built For The Future At A Decent Value Today
data.description:
Introduction

Match Group, Inc. (MTCH) stock has had a pretty average performance in the last year, with a very modest 1.9% rise. However, over the last five years, the stock's performance has been far less modest, with a decline of 79.5%. Because of this, the company’s shares are now trading at far cheaper levels than they were previously. They only have a forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 14.4x, compared with a five-year average of 32.1. With strong growth expected in the online dating market and Match Group being the leader in the industry, I see this as a great opportunity for investors to gain exposure to this by considering buying some of the company’s shares.

The Growing Online Dating Market

It should come as no surprise to readers that couples are increasingly meeting each other through online dating apps. For example, in the US in 2025, a recent study shows that over 50% of couples met through online dating. This is an increase from 39% in 2017. I anticipate that this will only grow as people spend more time on the internet and online. This is backed up by projections showing that the market is expected to grow by a compounded annual growth rate of 7.5% through to 2034.

Furthermore, as other countries and cultures start adopting online dating, this could boost revenue for the industry. For example, only 4.6% of people globally use dating apps. This could be a great opportunity for the companies behind the industry to take advantage of and to increase user penetration. As the largest online dating company in the world, I believe Match Group is best placed in this field. It owns 4 out of the top 10 most downloaded dating apps, putting it in such a competitively strong place.

Performance And Competitive Strengths

First, let's look at the underlying fundamentals of Match Group. If we look at the company’s latest earnings report, we can see that revenue increased by 2% year-on-year to $914 million in the third quarter of 2025. This is slightly concerning, given that it's lower than the anticipated growth for the industry, suggesting that it’s losing market share. Furthermore, its flagship app and highest source of revenue, Tinder, saw a 3% decline in its revenue to $491 million. These don’t bode well for the company. Moreover, there are concerns about the quality of online dating platforms, with researchers finding that people who met online were less happy than people who met in other ways.

I believe these are big reasons why Match Group has seen its share price fall so much over the last five years. However, not all is as bad as it seems. Its second-largest source of revenue, Hinge, is more than making up for its lost Tinder revenue. Its direct revenue shot up 27% to $185 million. Its paying subscribers also rose by 17% to 187 million. Having Hinge experience such strong growth is far better than having Tinder do so, as the average revenue per paying user (ARPU) of Hinge is $32.87 versus Tinder’s $17.66. Moreover, revenue from Tinder is also stabilizing, growing from $447 million in the first quarter of 2025 to $491 million in the latest quarter. This suggests that the app is in the process of making a turnaround.

From a competitive viewpoint, I believe Match Group still considerably dominates the online dating market scene. From a survey of which dating apps users used in 2025, 37% said they used Tinder, still higher than any other app. 21% used Plenty of Fish, 20% used Hinge, and 18% used Match.com, all also owned by Match Group and included in the top 10. With such a wide variety of platforms to use, I believe this diversification is very useful for the firm to cater for all different users of the platform in terms of what type of relationship they are looking for.

The company spent $50 million in the third quarter to innovate its features and try to expand its global footprint. This is in order to execute its turnaround in 2026 and 2027. If Hinge continues growing strongly, and Tinder continues stabilizing, I think the online dating provider will be in a great position to experience its turnaround, take advantage of its competitive position, and grow strongly as more people use online dating as a way to meet people.

Catalysts For A Turnaround

While the above analysis all sounds well and good, potential investors may be wondering why a turnaround may occur now, given that the outlook of estimates has been looking up for years, and it's still yet to be meaningful for Match Group. This may especially be a significant thought, given that young people are currently experiencing dating app fatigue. For example, 78% of people using dating apps have reported burnout from using them. However, it's important to note that this hasn't led to abandonment. Instead, it has led to users switching between apps. Therefore, those who may be fatigued by the big dating apps, like Tinder and Bumble, are turning to other ones, such as Hinge. This is backed up by the strong growth that Hinge is experiencing.

So while Tinder's growth may be disappointing, the real catalyst for the firm is Hinge. And even then, we can see that Tinder users are stabilizing. This is where Match Group's massive umbrella of dating apps is starting to become useful. Users may leave one Match Group app, like Tinder, but they are joining another, like Hinge. This creates a very effective defensive moat. Crucially, as mentioned above, Hinge's ARPU is much higher than Tinder's, presenting a real opportunity for the firm. Furthermore, the company is focusing on users developing a meaningful connection, such as implementing new features and using AI to improve match relevance. Hinge is already starting to use this, which is why we are seeing it be so successful.

With Hinge's quarterly revenue currently at $185 million, even if it maintained a 20% growth rate over the next two years (well below its current 27%), it could add an extra $326 million in incremental revenue annually. This is almost an additional 10% of revenue. And this is with a conservative estimate for its growth. This will be at a much higher margin, due to Hinge's much higher ARPU.

Moreover, even with dating app fatigue for young people, there's a very good opportunity for Match Group with respect to matchmaking for older generations. This is a demographic that's seeing increasing user numbers, with 12% of over 50's having used apps, rising to 30% for those aged 50-64. Match Group recently had two of its apps, Hinge and Match.com, listed in the top five best dating apps for older people. Older people are also more likely to pay for features on dating apps than younger people, with 44-45% of these users paying for these features compared to just 22% of those under 30. This means they are a more profitable demographic to target. If it can leverage this increasing popularity it has with older people, I think it can help to realise meaningful upside for its stock.

Peer Analysis And Valuation

I’m going to compare Match Group with Bumble Inc. (BMBL), its biggest competitor. Now, on initial inspection, it may look as though Match Group is more expensive than Bumble shares, as its forward P/E of 14.4x is far more than Bumble’s 1.5x. Moreover, Match Group’s forward price-to-sales ratio of 2.2x is also significantly higher than Bumble’s 0.4. However, I believe Match Group is highly deserving of this significant premium to Bumble. Match Group is significantly and consistently profitable, with a net income margin of 16.2%. Bumble, on the other hand, isn’t; it has a net income margin (19.8%). Match Group’s expected revenue growth of 2.2% over the next year is also superior to Bumble’s anticipated decline of 6.7%.

This shows that the company is performing much better than its closest competition. It therefore makes more sense to consider Match Groups' valuation independent of this, too. With the forward P/E of the S&P 500 at 22x, Match Group's forward P/E of 14.4x suggests some undervaluation. The company’s earnings per share are also set to grow by 17-23% over the next three years as well. This means its P/E in 2028 should fall to 8.3x based on current prices. Ultimately, I believe these points contribute to the undervaluation of the firm's shares.

Takeaway

My ultimate reasoning for Match Group’s shares being a good one for investors to consider is that the online dating market is set to grow strongly over the next decade, and that the company’s shares are cheap. I believe that because of growth drivers such as Hinge, it should be able to achieve its turnaround and grow well along with the rest of the industry. Its excellent competitive position and diversification should help it achieve this. Moreover, its shares have fallen significantly over the last five years, providing investors with an excellent price entry point to gain exposure to the online dating market through its shares.
